EIA-923
Ranking is built on actual generation, 2022–2025 — measured fact. Reported retirement dates are shown but never drive the rank, so reversals like Cumberland (capacity factor 40% and rising) correctly fall out of contention.

Where the targets are

Capacity-factor distribution

The proof-of-method vertical: the live global end-of-life market — every ocean-going ship dismantled worldwide in 2025, now underwritten on the scrap-floor thesis. Each vessel is scored on the cash floor its steel sets, the net recoverable value after deductions, and the max defensible entry price — the same discipline a PE buyer would apply before a single conversion dollar is spent.

Power lens: per-vessel conversion power requires installed main-engine output (kW/MW) — a Clarksons/IHS registry field not present in the demolition dataset. Flagged as the next data add; no per-ship MW is estimated here rather than invent one.

Each hull carries a net scrap floor (gross less tow, IHM, broker, yard and contingency) and a max-entry line at 80% of that floor. The score ranks vessels by floor attractiveness; conversion to floating power/compute is an option gated behind diligence — see each vessel's gate check. Scrap rates are a dated market quote (Go-Shipping Wk25, 19 Jun 2026), applied as a market range — per-vessel floors are modeled, not verified valuations: each rests on a weekly quote that moves ~30% a year and an LDT that needs confirming before use.

How vessel power compares — the cross-asset read

A scrapped hull isn't just steel — it's a pre-built, certified power asset. On cost of energy it loses; on time-to-power it wins decisively. That is the whole investment case, and it's why the floor matters: you enter through recoverable steel, not a bet on generation economics.

VesselFlagBuiltAgeLDTNet floorScoreScrapped at

Six ways to monetize a retiring vessel

Every hull has a floor — the question is what you do with it. Six monetization paths, each with a real-world champion already pursuing it, modeled on one consistent basis. The market keeps asking "can you put a data center on a ship"; the real questions are which path actually clears a return, where the downside sits when it doesn't, and when the conversion upside is worth firing. Below: the methods, the working model, the speed-to-power crossover, the whole comparison in one chart, and the verdict.

Single-asset illustrative models · 2026 USD · unlevered · pre-tax. Sources: Lazard LCOE+ 2025, CBRE H2 2025, JLL, GMS, BloombergNEF, Ship & Bunker, company disclosures. IRRs are illustrative, not forecasts.

③ When does a vessel beat the alternative? — the crossover

A vessel loses on raw cost of energy but wins on speed-to-power — so the real question is how much is avoiding a multi-year wait worth? Drag the levers; the model shows the threshold at which a vessel undercuts each alternative once you price in the revenue it brings forward.

④ Return vs. downside protection

The whole comparison in one frame. Higher is a better modeled return; further right is a stronger floor if the thesis fails. The methods in the shaded band clear both bars — real return and protected downside.

⑤ The conclusion

The third vertical: the end-of-life commercial jet, underwritten on the same discipline as the ships. Every U.S.-registered jet from the FAA Aircraft Registry, scored on its part-out floor — because the real downside on a retiring aircraft is not aluminum scrap, it is the documented value of its engines and traceable parts. — active jets analysed; — are 20+ years old, the teardown candidate pool.

Each aircraft carries a net USM floor (gross recoverable value less teardown, recertification and a slow-tail liquidation haircut) and a max-entry line at 55–70% of gross recoverable. Engines are 70–85% of a narrowbody's value — the score weights engine richness, green-time proxy and class liquidity. Part-out values are modeled from public teardown economics, not a live valuation feed.

What the live fleet is telling us

This is the part a generic teardown report can't give you: the strategy below is read directly off the live FAA registry — real tail numbers, real engine types, real operators. The thesis isn't "retire = scrap." It's engine-first: buy the aircraft at a discount to its net engine + module + USM recoverable value, monetise the engines now, and only convert when a contract makes the premium real.

Computed from the FAA Aircraft Registry candidate pool (top 3,000 by part-out score). Engine families normalised from the registry's engine field; part-out values modeled. Strategy economics: company disclosures and IBA/Cirium 2025–26 (FTAI, AAR, Willis Lease, GA Telesis, Satair-Unical, VAS, APOC, ProEnergy, FTAI Power, Boom). Figures are illustrative, not forecasts.

Where the recoverable value sits — by engine family

Aggregate modeled part-out value across the actionable pool. CFM56 alone is ~$4.7B — the part-out sweet spot — while CF6-80C2 is the exact core ProEnergy and FTAI Power convert to power.

Who holds the feedstock

Candidate aircraft are concentrated in a handful of operators and lessor trusts — named, addressable supply, not a diffuse market.

The $14B actionable pool is the summed modeled part-out value of the top 3,000 transport-category candidates (median age 29) — the aircraft a buyer can realistically target now. The ~$31B total-addressable figure scales to all 10,869 candidates 20+ years old, valuing the lower-scored tail at the pool's own bottom-decile (~$2.2M each) — a deliberately conservative extrapolation, not a forecast.

Four strategies companies are running right now

Not a textbook list — the actual 2025–26 plays, each with named operators and real unit economics. We've dropped the obvious passive options (scrap-only, speculative storage); they destroy value against any of these.

② Where the value is — by aircraft type

The single biggest driver is the green-time engine — remaining life before a major shop visit. Narrowbody with saleable CFM56/V2500 engines is the prime target; runout widebody is often oversupplied.

③ Return vs. downside floor

Higher is a better modeled return; further right is a stronger floor if the part-out tail is slow. The band clears both bars.

④ The conclusion

Why these assets — the thesis behind the screen
AI data centers need grid power years faster than the grid can deliver it — so the fastest way to get power is to buy something that already has it.
DEMAND
01
Data centers are stuck in line

A new grid connection now waits 3–7 years depending on market. Northern Virginia's data-center corridor is among the most congested. Queue-wait figures from LBNL "Queued Up 2025" and published industry sources.

SUPPLY
02
Retiring assets already own what's scarce

273 fading power plants (283 GW) hold grid connections that would take years to recreate. Ships and jets carry the same logic: hard floors set by steel and engines.

TRADE
03
Buy the infrastructure, not the business

Anchored to closed deals — Talen/AWS at $677k per MW (SEC-disclosed). At today's prices, plants clear the conversion bar at 1.99× break-even.

Explore the other verticals

About this demo

Asset Radar screens end-of-life and economically marginal assets — power plants, ships and jets — and ranks them as potential distressed acquisition or advisory targets. The power vertical ranks operating plants by measured utilization from EIA-923 generation data (2022–2025); a low and falling capacity factor means a plant is economically marginal and its grid interconnection is in play. The core thesis: a retiring plant's interconnection rights are often its most monetizable asset, because recreating that grid access takes years.

Sources. U.S. EIA-860 and EIA-923, LBNL “Queued Up 2025,” published capacity auction results, NGO Shipbreaking Platform, and the FAA Aircraft Registry. All public.

Public demo. This is a public demonstration build. The interconnection-scarcity overlay from the internal version is not included, and its column displays as “—”; base rankings are unaffected. Underwriting inputs — scrap-floor deductions, the entry discount and the gate thresholds — are this tool's own assumptions for demonstration, not validated firm methodology or market quotes. Nothing here is investment advice.

Snapshot

Point-in-time snapshots for demonstration, not live feeds. Figures are research signals, not investment advice; indicative values are illustrative.

Data: U.S. EIA-860 & EIA-923 (power) · LBNL "Queued Up 2025" (interconnection queues) · published capacity auction results · NGO Shipbreaking Platform 2025 & Go-Shipping demolition prices (ships) · U.S. FAA Aircraft Registry (jets).

Ask the analyst
Grounded in the thesis · offline · won't guess

The screener finds candidates. This tab answers the harder question: when does a legacy asset become worth more as data-center capacity than in its existing market? Each threshold below is anchored to a real, disclosed transaction (SEC filings / company releases), then applied back to every asset in the Radar. Not every old asset is stranded — conversion only pays when data-center pricing crosses the line.

The thesis in one line
Data-center power scarcity creates a new buyer for legacy assets — but each only works once market pricing crosses a specific, disclosed threshold.

The crossover thresholds — each anchored to a real deal

Anchor: Talen + Hallador (SEC)
Power plants
Break-even ~$150–200/MW-day. Hallador's filed $300/MW-day (2028) already clears it. Talen sold 960 MW to AWS for $677k/MW.
VERDICT: clears today — access is the constraint
Anchor: FTAI 10-K
Jet engines
Attractive above ~$1,200/kW for fast bridge power. FTAI earns a filed ~42% Aerospace margin doing exactly this with CFM56s.
VERDICT: crossover trade — works if bridge-power pricing is high
Anchor: CBRE + 5 evidence layers
Ships
Only clears if conversion is below ~$8–10M/MW at marine-risk WACC. No public conversion cost exists — the model solves for the ceiling.
VERDICT: quote-dependent — disciplined "not yet"

The crossover at a glance — price vs. break-even

Each bar is the real or supportable price as a multiple of that sector’s break-even. Above the dashed line (1.0×) the data-center use wins; below it, the asset is worth more in its existing market.

■ clears comfortably ■ clears with margin ■ just short — needs a sub-$10M/MW quote or a premium

Applied to the Radar — how many of your screened assets clear?

Computed live from the same data powering the other tabs. This is the bridge: the screener supplies the assets, the model supplies the rule.

The ranking, in plain English

# Sector What the model really says
1 Power plants Most economically proven (clears on filed prices), but most competitive and relationship-driven. The benchmark.
2 Jet engines Most differentiated crossover thesis. Works when bridge-power pricing is high enough to beat aviation value.
3 Ships Technically possible, quote-dependent. Only works if conversion capex is low or lease pricing is premium. Shows discipline, not forcing the thesis.
The conclusion
The model does not assume legacy assets are cheap or stranded. It tests when data-center power scarcity makes them more valuable in a new use than in their existing market.
On current thresholds: power plants already clear economically, jet engines become attractive if bridge-power pricing is high enough, and ships only work if conversion cost comes in below ~$8–10M/MW or the project captures a major speed-to-power premium.
Thresholds anchored to: Talen/AWS & Hallador (SEC) · FTAI 10-K · CBRE H2-2025 + MOL / Samsung-LR / Capital 20-F / Nautilus / DENV-R
